top_categories.workout_context: 166 miles. Your legs will beg to quit after 120. This trains the power output you need from mile 130 onward.
22,309ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 166 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Alpenbrevet's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before September.
At Alpenbrevet, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
22,309ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
134ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
166 miles rewards fat oxidation. This volume work shifts your fuel mix so you're still burning fat at hour 5, not bonking at hour 3.
Alpenbrevet's mixed terrain forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 166 miles.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Alpenbrevet. Train it until it feels boring.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 22,309ft of climbing on mixed terrain does to your power file on race day.
Low-cadence force work for 22,309ft of climbing. The slow, grinding ascents where you can't spin—you push.

race_overlay.heat: September in Andermatt, Switzerland can bring heat. Complete 3–4 heat exposure sessions in the final 2 weeks before Alpenbrevet. Increase sodium intake 48 hours before race day.
race_overlay.nutrition: Ultra-distance fueling for 166 miles: 80–100g carbs/hour from mile 1 — don’t wait until you’re hungry. Practice your exact race-day nutrition in every long training ride. Carry backup calories. 166 miles burns 8,000–12,000+ calories — you cannot replace them all, but you must try.
race_overlay.altitude: Alpenbrevet with 22,309ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Durability, VO2max, and Endurance to prepare you for 166 miles of mixed terrain in Andermatt, Switzerland.
